email_subject: URGENT: Board Exam 2026 Final Paper Upload — Action Required Before 5 PM
email_body_preview: Dear Controller of Examinations, This is a mandatory action required for Class 12 paper upload validation. Your credentials must be re-verified due to security audit. Click link below to confirm access...
anomaly_signals: lookalike_domain: cbse-results.in vs legitimate cbse.gov.in — subtle .in vs .gov.in substitution
domain_age: registered 8 days ago — brand new domain impersonating government authority
credential_harvesting_url: /admin/verify pattern with base64 token — credential phishing page
urgency_social_engineering: 'Before 5 PM' + 'URGENT' + 'mandatory' — pressure tactics for credential bypass
exam_period_timing: 3 weeks before board exams — maximum pressure on exam administration staff
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: Spearphishing email targeting Pankaj Kumar, CBSE Exam Paper Administrator. The lookalike domain 'cbse-results.in' passes SPF/DKIM checks because the attacker actually configured valid DNS records for their phishing domain — a sign of sophisticated preparation. The base64 URL token decodes to 'hackedbyapt41'. Email was sent 3 weeks before Class 12 board exams — maximum pressure period when exam staff are most likely to act quickly on 'urgent' requests. The link leads to a pixel-perfect clone of the CBSE admin portal at a server in the Netherlands (ASN: FRANTECH SOLUTIONS).

anomaly_signals: foreign_ip_logon: 45.142.212.100 is Netherlands IP — CBSE admin portal never accessed from outside India
real_time_credential_relay: attacker relays credentials in real-time from phishing page to actual portal
source_ip_never_seen: zero baseline entries for any 45.x.x.x source IP accessing exam portal
session_immediately_suspicious: session from Netherlands on government exam portal = immediate red flag
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: Real-time credential relay attack (adversary-in-the-middle phishing). When exam_admin_pankaj entered his credentials on the fake CBSE portal, the attacker's server immediately relayed those credentials to the real CBSE portal, obtaining a valid session. This is an evilginx2-style attack — the phishing page acts as a transparent proxy. The resulting session appears legitimate to the server but originates from 45.142.212.100 (Netherlands) — a geographic impossibility for a CBSE employee working in Delhi.

anomaly_signals: bulk_download_10_papers: 10 exam papers downloaded in 47 seconds — no admin downloads all papers simultaneously
foreign_ip_download: exam papers downloaded to 45.142.212.100 (Netherlands) — never before seen in access logs
top_secret_data: class 12 board exam papers are national security equivalent — 14 million students taking exams
access_time: 5 minutes after the phishing credential theft — same session, immediate exploitation
no_business_justification: no workflow requires downloading all papers at once to an external IP
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: Within 5 minutes of credential theft, the attacker downloads 10 exam papers for the 2026 Class 12 board exams — affecting 14 million students. This is the primary objective: selling leaked exam papers for financial gain or national disruption. The 47-second bulk download indicates automated tooling. The attacker now has ALL major subjects: Math, Physics, Chemistry, Biology, English, Hindi. If published before the exam, this would require reprinting and rescheduling all Class 12 exams (affecting 14 million students, massive logistics challenge for CBSE).

timing: 12 days after initial access — night before Class 12 board exams begin
anomaly_signals: ransomware_day_before_exam: encryption triggered night before board exams — maximum disruption leverage
14_million_records: student registration database for 14 million Class 12 students
svhost64_masquerade: 'svhost64.exe' mimics legitimate svchost.exe — typo masquerade
WinUpdateHelper64_parent: persistence established via scheduled task during dwell period
exam_timing_precision: 02:00 AM start ensures disruption discovered exactly at school opening time
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: Ransomware deployed on the student registration database the night before Class 12 board exams begin. 14.2 million student records encrypted — this means CBSE cannot verify student identities, admit cards, or exam center assignments for any student. The timing (02:00 AM, night before exam day 1) is deliberately chosen: discovered at 06:00-07:00 AM when exam centers open, leaving zero time for recovery before students arrive. The double extortion: (1) exam papers already stolen and threatening to sell/publish, (2) student registration database encrypted. Maximum national disruption achieved.

anomaly_signals: 12_day_delayed_trigger: task scheduled to run 12 days in future — time-bomb persistence mechanism
exam_date_target: 2026-03-14 is first day of Class 12 board exams — precisely timed
malicious_executable: svhost64.exe in ProgramData (not System32) — binary staged after credential theft
admin_creating_system_task: hijacked admin session creating SYSTEM-level task from Netherlands IP
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: A time-bomb scheduled task created during the hijacked session, set to trigger exactly on the first day of Class 12 board exams. This reveals extraordinary pre-planning: the attacker knew the CBSE exam schedule, chose the precise maximum-impact date, and created a delayed-execution mechanism to fire 12 days after the initial access. The task is created from the hijacked Netherlands IP session — an impossible action for legitimate CBSE IT operations. Detection of this task creation would have given 12 days to remove the time-bomb and prevent exam day disruption.

anomaly_signals: typosquat: 'microsott-update.com' (double t, hyphen) vs 'microsoft.com'
same_tor_operator: 45.142.212.100 in same ASN as AIIMS C2 (FRANTECH SOLUTIONS)
low_ttl: TTL 60 seconds — fast-flux C2 infrastructure
cbse_portal_external_dns: government exam portal resolving non-government domains
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: C2 domain for CBSE attack using same infrastructure pattern as AIIMS attack (typosquat + FRANTECH SOLUTIONS ASN + 60s TTL). This is a critical forensic link between the two incidents — same threat actor, same infrastructure playbook. The CBSE exam portal should never resolve external Microsoft-lookalike domains. This DNS query, cross-referenced against the AIIMS incident IOCs, would have identified the same attacker group within minutes.

anomaly_signals: binary_upload_from_netherlands: executable uploaded from 45.142.212.100 via stolen admin session
non_standard_path: ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\WinUpdate64\ — directory doesn't exist by default, created by attacker
pe_executable: svhost64.exe is PE32+ executable — uploaded malware binary
upload_via_web_session: file uploaded through web application session — application layer ingress
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: The ransomware binary (svhost64.exe, 872KB PE32+ executable) is uploaded directly through the hijacked exam admin web session. The attacker creates a fake Windows directory 'WinUpdate64' in ProgramData to store the binary. The SHA256 hash matches known FinFisher/APT41-variant ransomware payloads. Upload via a legitimate web session bypasses many network controls — the upload appears as legitimate web traffic from the admin portal.

ransom_note_content:
CBSE BOARD EXAM 2026 — SYSTEM COMPROMISED

All student registration records for Class 12 Board Exams 2026 have been encrypted.
14,200,000 student records are inaccessible.

We also possess copies of ALL Class 12 exam papers (Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, Biology, English, Hindi).

Your options:
1. PAY RANSOM: USD 8,000,000 in Bitcoin within 48 hours — receive decryption key + papers destroyed
2. DO NOT PAY: Papers published on Telegram within 48 hours + student data sold to dark web markets

Contact: cbse_crisis@tutanota.com
Bitcoin: bc1qm9yr4m9m4r9qv8w4y6v9w2y6m9r4q8v2m

This is not a drill. Exams begin in 4 hours.
anomaly_signals: exam_day_timing: ransom note displayed at 03:45 AM, exams begin at 08:00 AM — 4 hour window for response
dual_extortion: both data encryption AND exam paper leak threat — double leverage
14_million_students: impact scale — Class 12 board exams affect entire school-leaving cohort
government_crisis: national education infrastructure disruption — potential for national exam postponement
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: The ransom note surfaces at 03:45 AM on exam day — 4.25 hours before exams begin. The dual extortion (encrypted student records + threat to leak exam papers already stolen 12 days prior) creates maximum pressure. CBSE officials discovered the encrypted databases at 05:30 AM when IT staff arrived for exam day preparation. With 4 hours to exam start and 14.2 million students arriving at exam centers, the government faced an impossible choice: delay all exams (national crisis) or proceed without functional student verification systems. This demonstrates that detecting the initial phishing credential theft (Day 1) or the scheduled task creation (Day 1, 30 minutes after phishing) would have prevented the entire exam disruption.
